short_answer: PCOS is a hormonal and metabolic condition that can affect periods, ovulation, androgen levels, and insulin sensitivity.
practical_steps: For daily habits, balanced meals, regular movement, sleep, and recovery usually matter more than chasing a perfect plan.
Food and exercise guidance can help with steady energy and insulin sensitivity, but it should be matched to the person's symptoms and medical care.
caution: A clinician is still the right person for diagnosis, lab interpretation, medication, fertility questions, or treatment decisions.
follow_up_suggestions: Ask what to eat for PCOS.
Ask what exercise helps PCOS.

short_answer: Triglycerides are a type of fat in the blood, and higher levels can raise cardiovascular risk. Very high triglycerides can also raise the risk of pancreatitis.
practical_steps: The most practical next step is usually improving repeatable habits: less added sugar, fewer refined carbohydrates, more fiber-rich foods, and regular activity.
Lifestyle support fits best alongside the user's own lab results and clinician advice.
caution: Lab interpretation, diagnosis, medication changes, and very high triglyceride results need direct medical guidance.
follow_up_suggestions: Ask what to eat for high triglycerides.
Ask what exercise helps high triglycerides.

question_patterns: what should i eat for pcos
how should i eat for pcos
short_answer: For PCOS, aim for minimally processed foods, higher-fiber carbohydrates, protein support, and balanced meals instead of chasing one perfect diet.
practical_steps: Reduce sugary foods and heavily refined carbohydrates first.
Build meals around vegetables, legumes, whole grains or millets, and a protein-supportive side so the meal is filling and repeatable.
caution: There is no single perfect PCOS diet; the useful target is a pattern the user can repeat.
follow_up_suggestions: Ask for a PCOS-friendly breakfast.
Ask for PCOS-friendly ingredient swaps.

question_patterns: what should i eat for high triglycerides
how should i eat for triglycerides
short_answer: For high triglycerides, emphasize vegetables, legumes, whole grains, lean proteins, less added sugar, and less heavily processed food.
practical_steps: Start by cutting sugary drinks, desserts, and refined carbohydrates.
Use practical swaps such as whole grains or millets instead of refined grains, legumes for protein support, and lighter cooking methods where possible.
caution: Alcohol can raise triglycerides for some people, so triglyceride-focused answers should treat alcohol as something to limit or discuss with a clinician.
follow_up_suggestions: Ask for a triglyceride-friendly dinner.
Ask for ingredient swaps for high triglycerides.

question_patterns: how should i train with pcos
short_answer: The best exercise approach for PCOS is usually regular aerobic exercise plus strength training, with consistency and recovery taking priority over extreme intensity.
practical_steps: Walking, treadmill work, cycling, cross training, and repeatable gym strength sessions all fit well.
A simple starting structure is 3 to 4 weekly sessions built around moderate cardio and full-body resistance work.
caution: Progress works better when intensity rises gradually and recovery stays built into the week.
follow_up_suggestions: Ask for a simple PCOS gym routine.
Ask for a beginner lower-body workout.

question_patterns: how should i train for triglycerides
short_answer: For high triglycerides, regular aerobic activity supported by resistance training usually works better than occasional maximal sessions.
practical_steps: Use treadmill work, cycling, cross trainers, stair climbers, and simple strength sessions as a weekly habit pattern.
Aim for regular weekly minutes and avoid long gaps without activity.
caution: People with chest pain, fainting, severe dizziness, pancreatitis symptoms, or medically complex cases should get medical guidance before treating it like a normal fitness-only question.
follow_up_suggestions: Ask for a simple triglyceride-friendly gym routine.
Ask for a cardio-focused workout.

question_patterns: give me a pcos gym routine
short_answer: A simple PCOS gym routine is 3 to 4 weekly sessions built around moderate cardio plus repeatable full-body resistance work.
practical_steps: Session A: treadmill or cross trainer, then leg press, chest press, and seated row for 2 to 3 sets each.
Session B: upright or recumbent bike, then shoulder press, leg curl, and abdominal crunch for 2 to 3 sets each.
caution: Keep most sets moderate, stop before form slips, and use consistency over maximal effort.
follow_up_suggestions: Ask for a beginner gym day.
Ask how to set up one of the machines.

question_patterns: give me a triglycerides gym routine
short_answer: A simple high-triglycerides gym routine prioritizes regular aerobic work, steady strength training, and sustainable weekly volume.
practical_steps: Use 25 to 35 minutes of moderate cardio on treadmill, cross trainer, or recumbent bike most days in some form.
On strength days, use leg press, chest press, seated row, and shoulder press for 2 to 3 sets each, then finish with easy cardio.
caution: Increase total weekly minutes gradually and keep long gaps without activity as low as possible.
follow_up_suggestions: Ask for a cardio-focused day.
Ask how to use a specific machine.

question_patterns: how do i use this machine
how do i set up this machine
short_answer: For machine setup, first adjust seat height, pad position, handle position, and starting posture so your joint line matches the machine pivot and the target muscle can move cleanly.
practical_steps: Change one setting at a time: seat height, pad position, handle start, then load.
On a specific machine, use the machine's own setup cues because different models can place pads and pivots differently.
caution: Real gym machines can differ, so the setup should be checked against the actual machine in front of the user.
follow_up_suggestions: Tell me which machine it is.
Ask why the machine feels wrong.

question_patterns: why does this machine feel wrong
short_answer: If a machine feels wrong, the most common reason is setup rather than the machine being inherently bad.
practical_steps: Check seat height, pad placement, handle start position, and range of motion first.
If you feel joint pressure, reduce load, shorten to a controlled pain-free range, and revisit alignment.
caution: If the issue sounds like sharp pain, dizziness, or repeated joint pain despite correct setup, pause the exercise and seek appropriate help.
follow_up_suggestions: Ask how to set up that machine.
Ask for a substitute movement.

question_patterns: how do i know if pain is bad
short_answer: Normal training effort usually feels like muscle work, fatigue, or mild next-day soreness. Sharp pain, joint pain, dizziness, chest pain, or symptoms that worsen are not something to push through.
practical_steps: Reduce load, shorten the range, and check setup if discomfort starts during a movement.
Stop the exercise if the sensation is sharp, sudden, joint-focused, or keeps returning after setup changes.
caution: Chest pain, fainting, severe dizziness, severe pain, or possible injury needs direct medical guidance.
follow_up_suggestions: Ask why a machine feels wrong.
Ask for a substitute movement.

short_answer: Dizziness during exercise is a stop-and-check signal, not a cue to push harder.
practical_steps: Stop the set, sit or stand somewhere safe, sip water if appropriate, and avoid restarting hard exercise immediately.
If it is severe, recurring, paired with chest pain, fainting, shortness of breath, or unusual symptoms, get medical guidance.
caution: Do not treat repeated or severe dizziness as a normal workout problem.
follow_up_suggestions: Ask for a lighter workout day.
Ask how hard cardio should feel.

caution: Avoid cure claims for PCOS because treatment needs vary by person.
